tweets.full_text:
【衝撃】
海外でClaude Codeの具体的活用事例がまだ一切見つかっていない現実

注目ポイントはこれ
・企業名も開発者名もプロジェクトも数字付き成果もゼロ
・X上に公式もコミュニティも報告皆無

つまり
Claude Codeは「存在し使われている」けど
「具体的成功事例が見えない」状態が今続いている

これまでは
AIコード支援といえば実績報告がわりと豊富だったけど
今はClaude Codeの実用例は謎に包まれている

その感覚、かなり正常です。
期待値だけが先行する時代の真っ只中ですね

海外の開発現場は今まさに「使い方の地図」を模索中。
先に事例が出てくる前に、まずはClaude Codeの使い勝手を試して
自分のプロジェクトにどう組み込めるか動かしてみる一歩が大事です。

tweets.full_text:
夜11時。スマホでXを見てる。

フォロワー数万人のアカウントが「今月AI副業で80万達成！」って投稿してる。
いいね5000。「すごい！」「やり方教えて！」ってコメントが並んでる。

「俺もこの人みたいになれるかな」と思いながら、フォロー。

次の日。その人のプロフィールを見る。
「AI副業の仕組みを教えます。詳しくはLINE登録」。

LINE登録する。「無料動画を送ります」。見る。
最後に「本格的に学びたい方は月額3万円のコミュニティへ」。

「あ、そういう構造か」とようやく気づく。

俺も去年この流れにまんまと乗った。
月3万×6ヶ月＝18万。得たものは「AI副業は難しい」という誤解だけ。

今やってること：Claude Codeとスプレッドシート。月0円。
フォロワーは増え続けてる。

tweets.full_text:
昨日はClaude Codeを使いながら、製造業向けの外観検査システムのPoCを作成していた。

構成はこんな感じ。

画像
↓
C# HMI
↓
FastAPI
↓
AI判定
↓
JSON返却
↓
結果表示
途中でライブラリ不足やAPI連携エラー、multipart/form-dataの不整合など色々詰まったけど、一つずつ原因を潰して最終的に動作確認まで完了。
技術を学ぶだけなら記事や動画でもできる。
でも実際に作ると、
「どこでエラーになるのか」
「何が足りないのか」
がよく分かる。
やはり成長に一番効くのは手を動かすことだと思う。
#CSharp 
#Python 
#FastAPI 
#製造業DX 
#AI開発

user.description: 製造業×IT｜設備保全経験を活かし、C#・PLC・Modbus TCP・HMIを学習・開発中。製造業DX、設備監視、IoTに興味。将来は地元でIT企業を立ち上げるのが目標。

tweets.full_text:
シリコンバレーで「AI Forward Deployed Engineer」って役職が増えてる。

簡単に言うと、クライアント企業に入り込んで、その会社専用のAIエージェントを作って、チューニングして、回し続ける人。

これを聞いて最初は「やっぱりエンジニア向けの話か」と思ったんだけど、よく考えたら違った。

現場の業務を理解してる人間がいないと、エージェントはまず使えないレベルで崩壊する。
実際に僕もClaude CodeやCursorでエージェント的な動きを作ろうとして、何度も「業務の文脈」が抜けてる部分でつまずいた。

技術よりも「この現場でどう動かすか」を知ってる人間の価値が上がってきてる気がする。

user.name: てんそ｜医療× AI
user.location: 🔻医療職用ChatGPTプロンプト公開中🔻
user.description: 大学病院20年勤務の医療職がClaude Codeで現場業務を自動化実践中｜勤務表アプリ作成で80%効率化を実現｜研修資料・マニュアル作成をAIで半自動化に挑戦中｜生成AIパスポート取得｜AIコーチ・講演実績あり｜医療現場をラクにするAI活用を発信

tweets.full_text:
普通のAIエージェントって、検索するたびに「関数呼び出し→結果取得→次の呼び出し→…」を繰り返す構造になってる。

これ、タスクが複雑になるほど遅くなるし、途中でズレていくことも多い。

Search as Codeは、Pythonでそのまま検索スタックを直接呼び出せる設計になってる。
ループを1個ずつ回すんじゃなくて、「まとめて処理しちゃう」感じ。

Claude CodeやCursorでエージェント的な使い方してると、この「ループ地獄」に何度もハマったことがあって。
この発想は素直に「あ、そこを直しに来たか」って思った。

まだ自分の手元で本格的に試せてないんだけど、Perplexity Agent APIで使えるらしい。

tweets.full_text:
Side Project - Build Manager: Native macOS app that runs a coordinated AI swarm.

Dispatch a brief. Boss Builder decomposes the work, spins up Grok Build + Claude Code workers, best of both worlds. Monitors completions, de-provisions when done. You approve the plan, the swarm does the rest.

Every completed build gets audited by two fresh sessions — independent eyes, independent verdicts. The results feed a local intelligence store, for self improvement. Routing gets smarter with every build.

Full power of both Grok and Claude.

tweets.full_text:
On the list of things to do this week:

Meta released **Meta Ads AI Connectors** (powered by their official **Ads Model Context Protocol / MCP server**) in open beta around late April 2026. It’s designed exactly for letting AI agents (like Claude Code and Codex, etc.) securely connect to your Meta ad account for natural-language tasks.

- **Official MCP server URL**: `https://t.co/mGiYtlvlMw`
- It supports:
  - Pulling detailed reports & insights
  - Creating/editing campaigns, ad sets, and ads
  - Catalog/product feed management
  - Signal diagnostics (pixel/health checks)
  - Troubleshooting help
- Write actions (e.g., creating or changing campaigns) require your explicit authorization in the AI interface.
- No custom API keys or heavy dev setup needed — just authenticate via Meta OAuth.
### Why you might not have seen/found it easily:

- It’s still in **gradual rollout** (phased by ad account). Not every account has it enabled yet and many users see messages like “This ad account is not enabled for the Ads MCP” and are told to check back later.
- There are also third-party/open-source MCP servers for Meta Ads (some existed before Meta’s official one), which can add confusion.

### How to check / set it up:

1. Go to your preferred AI tool that supports MCP (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.).
2. Add the remote MCP server: `https://t.co/mGiYtlvlMw`
3. Authenticate with your Meta ad account.
4. Test if your specific ad account is enabled.

If it’s not available for your account yet, that’s common right now, you have to wait.

tweets.full_text:
One Claude Code command can turn 20 research PDFs into a NotebookLM briefing and drop it straight into Obsidian.

Setup: 

> open Claude Code inside your Obsidian vault 
> run /plugin 
> install skill-creator 
> restart Claude Code 
> create /doc-ingest with pypdf 
> install notebooklm-py > run notebooklm login 
> create a NotebookLM skill 
> create one combined /research-pipeline skill

What /research-pipeline does: 

> scans a folder of PDFs, papers, or saved links 
> pulls titles, authors, dates, and key claims 
> sends every source into a fresh NotebookLM notebook 
> asks NotebookLM for a structured synthesis 
> optionally asks for infographic, mindmap, flashcards, or audio 
> writes the final markdown report into your Obsidian vault

Example command:

> /research-pipeline analyze the state of AI agents in 2026. Pull my 20 saved PDFs, compare architectures, adoption, open problems, and contradictions between sources. Generate an infographic.

Swap PDFs later for: 

> YouTube transcripts 
> arXiv papers 
> SEC filings 
> earnings calls 
> Google Drive docs 
> public web pages

First build /doc-ingest, then NotebookLM, then /research-pipeline.  

The pipeline is the product. 

The sources are just placeholders.

9/ Set up hooks

Hooks are a way to deterministically hook into Claude's lifecycle. Use them to:
- Automatically route permission requests to Slack or Opus
- Nudge Claude to keep going when it reaches the end of a turn (you can even kick off an agent or use a prompt to decide whether Claude should keep going)
- Pre-process or post-process tool calls, eg. to add your own logging

Ask Claude to add a hook to get started.

Learn more: https://t.co/GukTJ9iy0R

tweets.full_text:
if you're a pm who spent this week reading about claude code dynamic workflows, you saw the same headline numbers i did. up to 1,000 agents, 16 running at once, javascript scripts orchestrating the whole thing. everyone is arguing about the tool. almost nobody is asking the question that matters.

the question is not whether it's powerful. it's obviously powerful. the question is whether your work is in a shape it can run on.

every time a tool like this drops, the discourse splits into the same camps. is this better than the old way. is the javascript layer overkill. should you run 16 agents or is that chaos. how many of the 1,000 will you need.

and all of it is BS, because every one of those questions is about the tool when the problem is the work. the tool debate is comfortable. it lets you stay in consumer mode, evaluating features, comparing specs, waiting for someone to declare a winner. meanwhile nobody is looking at the real problem, which is that most pm work is a pile of vibes and meeting context that no orchestration layer can touch.

junior pm: okay but dynamic workflows can run many agents at once. that's the part that matters, right?

senior pm: it can. now tell me the things you'd hand them.

junior pm: well, i'd have one do the strategy, one do the market research, one work on the roadmap...

senior pm: stop. "do the strategy" is not a task. it's a prayer.

that exchange is the whole article. the tool is ready. your work is not.

the part the feature announcements gloss over: dynamic workflows is an orchestration layer. it fans work out, runs pieces in parallel, gathers results back. it coordinates tasks. it does not invent the tasks for you.

so it needs something to coordinate. it needs work broken into pieces with clear inputs and clear outputs. it needs steps that can run without a human sitting in the loop explaining what they meant. it needs a system to run on.

for engineers this is not a problem (which is why the feature landed in their world first). a codebase is already decomposed. modules, functions, tests, files with defined inputs and outputs. when an engineer says "refactor the auth layer and run the test suite across these 16 packages," the work is already in orchestratable units. the agents have somewhere to land.

pm work is almost never in that shape.

think about how pm work usually lives. it lives in your head, in half-written docs, in the context of three meetings you attended and one you missed, in a sense of the company you carry around but never wrote down.

when you tell an ai to "research the market," you are not giving it a task. you are giving it a category. no defined input, no sequence of steps, no description of what done looks like. so the model guesses, and you get something that reads fine and means nothing specific to your company.

junior pm: but i could write a really detailed prompt. like, three paragraphs explaining what i want.

senior pm: you could. and you'd write it again next week from scratch, slightly different, because it lived in a chat window that's gone now.

junior pm: ...so the prompt isn't the unit.

senior pm: right. a prompt is a sentence you throw away. a skill is a unit you keep. dynamic workflows can orchestrate the second one. it can't do anything with the first.

the bottleneck for pms using these orchestration tools is not the tool and it's not access. it's that the work itself has never been decomposed into units anything could orchestrate. you could have 10,000 agents and it wouldn't matter.

so the tool debate dissolves once you ask the better question. it stops being "which ai tool is best for pms" and becomes "is my work broken into pieces that any tool could run."

if your work is decomposed, you have options. orchestrate it with dynamic workflows, or the next thing, or the thing after that. if your work is not decomposed, the most powerful orchestration engine in the world has nothing to grab. it's 1,000 agents staring at a blank prompt.

decomposition means three boring things for every piece of work. what goes in. what steps run, in what order. what comes out, in a shape you can check. three requirements, and most pms haven't done any of them for their own job.

i spent a long time turning pm work into exactly these units because i wanted my own work to be orchestratable and it wasn't

the result is pm os. 11 workflows and 235 skills. every skill has defined inputs, a sequence of steps, and a defined output. every workflow chains those skills in a kind of a lattice, so "do strategy" becomes a real sequence instead of a wish.

"research the market" stops being a category. it becomes the research-to-feature workflow, which calls specific skills in order, each one taking a defined input and producing a defined output the next step can use. "review this decision" becomes the multi-perspective review, which fans out across reviewer agents (engineering, design, executive, legal, devil's advocate) and gathers their reads back. that fan-out-and-gather shape is precisely what an orchestration layer was built to run.

on one side: 11 workflows and 235 skills with inputs and outputs, sitting ready to be orchestrated. on the other side: one blank prompt and good intentions. dynamic workflows can do something extraordinary with the first and damn near nothing with the second.

you could, in theory, do this yourself. write the skills. define the inputs and outputs. chain them into workflows. i did it, and it took months, because the hard part is not any single skill. the hard part is the boring, total work of getting all of it into a consistent shape that an orchestrator can trust.

most pms will not do that, for the same reason most of us never wrote down our company context (the calendar always wins). so the realistic move, if you want your work in orchestratable shape before the next tool drops, is to start from a system where the decomposition is done for you.
